name: Puente de Megeces from Arrabal de Portillo – circular via Mojados
name_original: Puente de Megeces desde Arrabal de Portillo – circular por Mojados
region_intro: Road cycling around Portillo is characterized by rolling agricultural landscapes, historic towns, and routes that often follow the Duero river. The terrain typically features gentle ascents and descents through cultivated fields and vineyards. This region offers a network of roads suitable for various levels of road cyclists, connecting local villages and historical sites. Best road cycling routes around Portillo The most popular road cycling route is Portillo Castle and La Parrilla Loop, a 24.2 miles (39.0 km) trail that takes 1 hour 41 minutes to complete. This route offers views of local landmarks and the surrounding countryside. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Puente de Megeces from Arrabal de Portillo – circular via Mojados, a moderate 40.5 miles (65.1 km) path. This route traverses the landscape between villages, featuring open views and varied terrain. Local road cyclists also love the Cogeces del Monte and Peñafiel from Tudela de Duero – circular by the Duero river, a 63.9 miles (103.0 km) trail leading through river valleys and past vineyards, often completed in about 4 hours 13 minutes. Road cycling around Portillo is defined by agricultural plains, riverine paths along the Duero, and routes connecting historic towns and vineyards. The network offers options for different ability levels, from shorter loops to longer excursions. More than 250 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Portillo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Portillo?
A: Road cycling around Portillo is characterized by rolling agricultural landscapes, historic towns, and routes that often follow the Duero river. The terrain typically features gentle ascents and descents through cultivated fields and vineyards, offering a network of roads suitable for various levels of road cyclists.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Portillo area?
A: There are over 15 road cycling routes available around Portillo, catering to different skill levels. These routes connect local villages and historical sites, providing diverse options for exploration.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes for beginners in Portillo?
A: Yes, Portillo offers several easier road cycling routes. There are 6 routes classified as 'easy', perfect for those new to road cycling or looking for a more relaxed ride through the agricultural plains and historic towns.

Q: What are some moderate road cycling routes around Portillo?
A: For those seeking a moderate challenge, there are 12 routes available. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170676035/puente-de-megeces-from-arrabal-de-portillo-circular-via-mojados" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de Megeces from Arrabal de Portillo – circular via Mojados&lt;/a&gt;, which is 40.5 miles (65.1 km) long and traverses varied terrain between villages.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for advanced riders?
A: Yes, for advanced riders looking for a significant challenge, there is at least one difficult route. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1353858334/portillo-and-duero-vineyards-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portillo and Duero Vineyards Loop&lt;/a&gt;, for example, covers over 109 km with nearly 900 meters of elevation gain, offering a demanding ride through the region's vineyards and landscapes.

Q: What landmarks or attractions can I see along the road cycling routes in Portillo?
A: Many routes pass by historical and natural attractions. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5838613" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portillo Castle&lt;/a&gt;, visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5780020" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Juan Church in Mojados&lt;/a&gt;, or cross the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5597863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Megeces Bridge&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes also follow the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2689667" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cega River Trails&lt;/a&gt;, offering scenic views.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Portillo?
A: Yes, many of the routes in Portillo are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1175802110/portillo-castle-and-la-parrilla-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portillo Castle and La Parrilla Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the longer &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1169216587/cogeces-del-monte-and-penafiel-from-tudela-de-duero-circular-by-the-duero-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cogeces del Monte and Peñafiel from Tudela de Duero – circular by the Duero river&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Portillo?
A: Road cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from agricultural plains to riverine paths along the Duero, and the routes connecting historic towns and vineyards. The region's network offers options for different ability levels, making it enjoyable for many.

Q: Can I find routes that follow the Duero river?
A: Absolutely. Several routes are designed to follow the Duero river, offering picturesque views and often leading through vineyards. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170326280/olivares-de-duero-from-tudela-de-duero-circular-of-the-duero-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olivares de Duero from Tudela de Duero – circular of the Duero river&lt;/a&gt; is a great example of a route that highlights the river's beauty.

Q: What is the longest road cycling route available in Portillo?
A: One of the longest routes is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1353858334/portillo-and-duero-vineyards-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portillo and Duero Vineyards Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which spans approximately 109.4 kilometers (68 miles). This route offers an extensive exploration of the region's landscapes and vineyards.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through vineyards?
A: Yes, the region is known for its vineyards, especially along the Duero river. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1353858334/portillo-and-duero-vineyards-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portillo and Duero Vineyards Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1169216587/cogeces-del-monte-and-penafiel-from-tudela-de-duero-circular-by-the-duero-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cogeces del Monte and Peñafiel from Tudela de Duero – circular by the Duero river&lt;/a&gt; will take you through beautiful vineyard landscapes.
tips:
Valladolid town where we find two beautiful churches built by mixing brick and masonry.
The Church of San Juan, Mudejar from the 13th and 14th centuries, in which its beautiful tower stands out, and the doorway with a pointed horseshoe arch.
The other church, very close to the path of the Cega river, is that of Santa María, Mudejar and from the 14th century.

The bridge over the Cega River, built between 1515 and 1516, is a remarkable structure commissioned by the Archpriest of Cogeces, Juan Díaz de Mirena. It consists of a single limestone semicircular arch and is twelve meters long. In the lower part, it has buttresses, and its railing is decorated with large semicircular stones. Inside the arch, fragments of the old wooden framework used during its construction are preserved.
Lonely road with bad asphalt that runs through a leafy pine forest in the mountains
On a fortified hill stands the Castillo de Portillo. The climb to the castle is steep but with incredible views that will greet you from the top of its defensive walls.
